text: IN THE COURT OF APPEAL OF THE DEMOCRATIC SOCIALIST
In the matter of an application for Restitution, in the nature of Restitutio-In-Integrum under and in terms of Article 138(1) of the Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka.

Court of Appeal Case No: RII/0003/2024 Kalutota Investment Private Limited No. 49 Hudson Road Colombo 03 Plaintiff WP/HCCA/LA/MT/04/2023 VS DC Nugegoda Case No: L/734/18 Kumudu Samanthi Akmeemana No. 601, Pirivena Road Boralesgamuwa Defendant AND THEN BETWEEN Kumudu Samanthi Akmeemana No. 601, Pirivena Road, Boralesgamuwa Defendant-Petitioner Vs. Kalutota Investment Private Limited No. 49 Hudson Road, Colombo 03 Plaintiff-Respondent And Now Between Kumudu Samanthi Akmeemana No. 601, Pirivena Road, Boralesgamuwa Defendant-Petitioner-Petitioner

Vs. Kalutota Investment Private Limited No. 49 Hudson Road, Colombo 03 Plaintiff-Respondent-Respondent Before : R. Gurusinghe, J. & Dr. S. Premachandra, J. Counsel : Upul Jayasuriya, P.C. with Sandamal Rajapakse and Kalpanee Dissnayake for the Petitioner Sumedha Mahawanniarachchi Instructed by M. Rupasinghe for the Respondent Supported on : 14-01-2026 Decided on : 20-03-2026

ORDER R. Gurusinghe, J. When this matter was taken up for hearing, the Learned Counsel for the Plaintiff-respondent (the plaintiff) raised a preliminary objection on the basis that the defendant-petitioner (the defendant) had made an application in the Civil Appellate High Court of Mount Lavinia. It was further submitted that the proper course available for the defendant-petitioner was to make a Leave to Appeal application in the Supreme Court. He further, submitted a recent judgment of the Supreme Court in support of his submissions. In W.T.F. Nilantha Fernando vs P.N.S. Nilanthi Perera SC/Appeal/65/2025, the Supreme Court held that “the Court of Appeal has no jurisdiction whether by way of Final Appeal, Revision, or Restitutio-in-Integrum to relieve the judgments or Orders of the Provincial High Court whether in the exercise of its Appellate Jurisdiction under Act No. 19 of [1990], as Amended by Act No. 54 of [2006], or in the exercise of its original

jurisdiction under Act No. 10 of [1996]. Such jurisdiction is vested exclusively in the Supreme Court.” The Learned Counsel for the defendant-petitioner stated that the present matter is with regard to a settlement, and that the plaintiff, having accepted the monies after the period stipulated in the original settlement, cannot go back and seek to rely upon the time frame of the original settlement.

The defendant petitioner has executed a Deed of Transfer in favour of the plaintiff company and obtained a loan of Rs. 1,500,000/-. Thereafter, there was an application before the Debt Conciliation Board, where the parties reached at a settlement stipulating that the defendant-petitioner was obliged to pay Rs. 3,900,000/- to the plaintiff representing the capital and interest. Upon payment of the said amount, the plaintiff was to execute a Deed of Transfer in favour of the defendant-petitioner. The plaintiff instituted a declaration of title and ejectment action against the defendant-petitioner in respect of the same property. The defendant took up the position that, as the matter was already pending before the Debt Conciliation Board, and in terms of Section 56 of the Debt Conciliation Board Ordinance, the plaintiff’s action could not be proceeded in the District Court. However, after the recording of admissions and issues, the parties reached a settlement. The written settlement comprised of seven written terms signed by the plaintiff, the defendant, and their respective registered Attorneys.

According to the terms of settlement, the defendant agreed to pay a sum of Rs. 3,900,000/- to the plaintiff. It was further agreed by the parties that out of this Rs. 3,900,000/-, a sum of Rs. 1,400,000/- had already been paid to the plaintiff, and the remaining balance of Rs. 2,500,000/- was to be paid within one year. Upon payment of the balance sum of Rs. 2,500,000/-, the plaintiff was obliged to execute a Deed of Transfer in favour of the defendant. The defendant agreed to bear the stamp fees and notary fees for the said deed. Further, to secure the payment of Rs. 2,500,000/-, the parties agreed that the plaintiff would retain the registration certificate and absolute ownership of the vehicle bearing No. PY 7441. Upon the settlement of payment in full, the plaintiff agreed to cancel the absolute ownership in favour of the defendant. Further, in the event where the defendant failed to pay the balance sum of Rs. 2,500,000/- as agreed, the defendant was obliged to hand over the possession of the property to the plaintiff.
In addition to the agreed seven terms of settlement, an additional term was introduced, making eight terms in total, on 16-09-2022. There was a

dispute that subsequently arose as to the eighth term of settlement. The said dispute was the matter before the High Court of Civil Appeals. The Civil Appellate High Court did not decide any issue as the Learned District Judge had not yet made any order regarding the validity of clause eight. In the above circumstances, one cannot argue that the Civil Appellate High Court has decided the matter. It must also be noted that the present application is not about clause eight of the terms of the settlement. The defendant petitioner has failed to make the full payment as agreed. According to the terms of settlement, payment was required to be completed on 15-09-2021. However, the plaintiff has accepted a sum of Rs. 1,000,000/- from the defendant on 08-01-2022. Thereafter, on 03-09-2022, the plaintiff accepted a further payment of Rs. 200,000/- followed by an additional sum of Rs. 100,000/- on 12-09-2022. Accordingly, out of the total sum of Rs. 3,900,000/-, the defendant has paid up to Rs. 2,700,000/-, leaving a balance of Rs. 1,200,000/-. The plaintiff filed papers before the District Court seeking to obtain possession of the property. Now the matter before this Court is whether the plaintiff can obtain possession of the land. The defendant-petitioner seeks a direction from the Court to the plaintiff, to accept the balance sum of money according to the terms of the settlement. In the above circumstances, the issue before this Court is not a matter that has been decided by the Civil Appellate High Court. Therefore, the preliminary objections taken up by the plaintiff-respondent are overruled. The matter will be fixed for hearing on merit.
Judge of the Court of Appeal. Dr. S. Premachandra J. I agree. Judge of the Court of Appeal.
